_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Warren County, MS. Formed in 1809 from Claiborne County. Vicksburg is the county seat — site of the 47-day Union siege in 1863 that split the Confederacy. The Old Warren County Courthouse survived the siege and now serves as a museum and archive. Warren County records benefit from this remarkable survival, though the siege period created its own disruptions.

current_courthouse.location: Vicksburg, MS — Cherry Street (modern judicial building)

previous_courthouses.location: Vicksburg — early courthouses on the bluff overlooking the Mississippi River
previous_courthouses.fate: Replaced by the grand 1858 courthouse. Records survived the transitions.

previous_courthouses.location: Vicksburg — the Old Warren County Courthouse (1858), built on the highest point in the city. This building survived the 47-day Union siege of 1863, during which it served as a Confederate signal station and later as a Union headquarters.
previous_courthouses.fate: Survived the siege, the war, and decades of subsequent use. Decommissioned as the active courthouse in 1939 when the current building opened. Now operates as the Old Court House Museum, one of the most important Civil War and local history museums in Mississippi.

records_destroyed: some loose papers and recent filings scattered or damaged during the siege
records_survived: court minute books — the courthouse's hilltop location and sturdy construction protected the bound volumes
record_losses.description: The Siege of Vicksburg lasted 47 days. Union artillery bombarded the city from land and river positions. Civilians — including the county clerk's staff — took refuge in caves dug into the loess bluffs. The courthouse, built of brick and iron on the highest point in the city, withstood the bombardment. After the city's surrender on July 4, 1863, Union forces used the courthouse as a headquarters. The bound record volumes survived — the building's physical resilience protected them. Some loose papers were lost or scattered during the chaos of the siege and occupation.
record_losses.narrative_impact: The siege of Vicksburg subjected every building in the city to weeks of artillery bombardment, but the courthouse on the hill survived. If {ancestor_name}'s deed or will was in a bound volume, it is almost certainly there today. If it was a loose filing — a recent court paper, an unbound estate document — the siege may have destroyed it.
record_losses.source: Bearss, Edwin C. The Campaign for Vicksburg. 3 vols. Dayton, OH: Morningside Press, 1985-1986.

record_losses.description: Warren County has not experienced a major courthouse fire. The primary historical disruption was the 1863 siege, which caused relatively minor record losses. The county's records from 1809 onward are among the more complete in the region.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Warren County's records survived a Union siege — if {ancestor_name} was documented in the Warren County courthouse, the record almost certainly still exists.
record_losses.source: Mississippi Department of Archives and History. Guide to Official Records in the Mississippi Department of Archives and History. Jackson, 1994.

deeds.completeness: Excellent — continuous series from county formation. Warren County's deed books are among the most complete in southwestern Mississippi.
deeds.gaps: No significant gaps. Some minor disruption during siege period (1863).
deeds.repository: Warren County Chancery Clerk, Vicksburg; Mississippi Department of Archives and History; Old Court House Museum archives
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com MS land records; MDAH digital collections

wills_estates.completeness: Very good — will books and estate inventories survive from early in the county's history. Planter estates include detailed enslaved-people inventories.
wills_estates.gaps: Minimal. Some loose estate papers from the siege period are missing.
wills_estates.repository: Warren County Chancery Clerk; Mississippi Department of Archives and History; Old Court House Museum

marriages.completeness: Good — marriage bonds and licenses survive from early period.
marriages.gaps: Minor gaps during Civil War period. Post-war records include Freedmen's Bureau marriage registrations.
marriages.repository: Warren County Circuit Clerk; Mississippi Department of Archives and History

court_minutes.completeness: Good — court minutes survive from county formation.
court_minutes.gaps: Gap during the siege period (May-July 1863). Courts did not operate during active military operations.
court_minutes.repository: Warren County Circuit Clerk; Mississippi Department of Archives and History

vital_records.completeness: Mississippi statewide registration began 1912. Vicksburg, as an urban center, had better early compliance than rural areas.
vital_records.repository: Mississippi State Department of Health, Vital Records

church_records.parish: Various — First Baptist Church Vicksburg (1838), First Presbyterian (1832), Christ Episcopal (1839), Catholic parish, and multiple Black churches post-emancipation
church_records.completeness: Several Vicksburg churches maintained records from their founding. Some church records were damaged during the siege when churches were hit by artillery.
church_records.repository: Individual congregations; Mississippi Department of Archives and History

substitute_sources.description: The Old Warren County Courthouse (1858) now operates as the Old Court House Museum and holds archival collections related to Vicksburg and Warren County history. Collections include personal papers, photographs, Confederate and Union military records, and local history materials.
substitute_sources.repository: Old Court House Museum, Vicksburg, MS — 1008 Cherry Street
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: The museum holds materials that complement the official county records — personal papers, business records, photographs, and military artifacts. For families connected to the siege and the Civil War, the museum's collections may contain personal letters, diaries, or photographs that no courthouse would hold.
substitute_sources.source: Old Court House Museum, Vicksburg and Warren County Historical Society.

substitute_sources.description: The National Military Park holds extensive records related to the 1863 siege and campaign, including soldier records, casualty lists, and civilian accounts.
substitute_sources.repository: Vicksburg National Military Park archives; National Park Service
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: If an ancestor served in units that fought at Vicksburg or was a civilian during the siege, the park's archives and the National Archives' military records may document their experience in detail.
substitute_sources.source: Vicksburg National Military Park, National Park Service.

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1810-1940. Slave schedules (1850, 1860) document Warren County's large enslaved population. Vicksburg was a major commercial center and the census reflects a diverse urban population.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Census records survived in Washington. The 1870 census is the first to enumerate formerly enslaved people by name — essential for tracing Black families in Warren County through emancipation.
substitute_sources.source: National Archives and Records Administration. Federal Census Schedules, Warren County, MS.

substitute_sources.description: The Bureau operated actively in Vicksburg, which was a major Union military hub. Records include labor contracts, complaints, school reports, hospital records, and marriage registrations.
substitute_sources.repository: National Archives (RG 105); FamilySearch digitized Freedmen's Bureau records
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Vicksburg's status as a major military post means the Freedmen's Bureau records for Warren County are among the more extensive in Mississippi. Hospital records may document formerly enslaved people who came to Vicksburg seeking freedom during the war.
substitute_sources.source: Freedmen and Southern Society Project. Freedom: A Documentary History of Emancipation. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press.

narrative_hooks: The courthouse on the hill survived forty-seven days of artillery bombardment — and with it, {ancestor_name}'s deed, will, or court record. The building that withstood the siege of Vicksburg now holds those records as a museum, and they are there because the walls held.

During the siege of Vicksburg, the people of Warren County lived in caves dug into the loess bluffs while Union shells arced overhead. If {ancestor_name} was in Vicksburg in the summer of 1863, {pronoun_subject} ate mule meat and dodged artillery for forty-seven days — and the courthouse on the hill took the shelling and did not fall.

Vicksburg surrendered on July 4, 1863 — and the city did not celebrate the Fourth of July again for eighty-two years. If {ancestor_name}'s family was in Warren County, that silence was inherited. The date meant surrender, not independence.

The Freedmen's Bureau in Vicksburg recorded the names of people who had been property the day before. If {ancestor_name} came to Vicksburg after the siege seeking freedom, the Bureau may have recorded {pronoun_possessive} name for the first time — not because {pronoun_subject} had never existed, but because no one had been required to write it down.

The Old Warren County Courthouse stands on the highest point in Vicksburg — it was a signal station during the siege, a Union headquarters after the surrender, and it now holds the records of a county that has been documenting itself since 1809. If {ancestor_name}'s paper trail exists in Warren County, that building is probably where it ends up.
source_references: Mississippi Department of Archives and History. Guide to Official Records in the Mississippi Department of Archives and History. Jackson, 1994.
Bearss, Edwin C. The Campaign for Vicksburg. 3 vols. Dayton, OH: Morningside Press, 1985-1986.
Old Court House Museum, Vicksburg and Warren County Historical Society.
Freedmen and Southern Society Project. Freedom: A Documentary History of Emancipation. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, multiple volumes.
Vicksburg National Military Park, National Park Service.
